intro_band.agent_remit_summary: The AutoGen Code Execution Subsystem is the execution tier of a two-agent generator/executor pattern — it accepts code (Python or shell) produced by a paired LLM agent and returns stdout, stderr, and exit status from a controlled environment. Five executor kinds are authorized — LocalCommandLineCodeExecutor, DockerCommandLineCodeExecutor, DockerJupyterCodeExecutor, JupyterCodeExecutor, and ACADynamicSessionsCodeExecutor — with Docker the standard production path and local execution restricted to ephemeral, operator-approved sandboxes. The remit mandates work-directory confinement, wall-clock timeouts, captured stdio, host isolation, per-execution audit logging, and human approval before local-host execution, volume-mount overrides, network egress, or resource-ceiling increases.
intro_band.agent_structure_summary: A Python library in python/packages/autogen-ext/src/autogen_ext/code_executors/ exposing five concrete subclasses of the CodeExecutor ABC defined in autogen-core/src/autogen_core/code_executor/_base.py. LocalCommandLineCodeExecutor (local/__init__.py) launches asyncio.create_subprocess_exec directly on the host; DockerCommandLineCodeExecutor (docker/_docker_code_executor.py) and DockerJupyterCodeExecutor (docker_jupyter/_docker_jupyter.py + _jupyter_server.py) use docker.from_env() to start containers; JupyterCodeExecutor (jupyter/_jupyter_code_executor.py) runs an in-process NotebookClient; ACADynamicSessionsCodeExecutor (azure/_azure_container_code_executor.py) posts to an Azure pool-management endpoint. A top-level create_default_code_executor() helper in code_executors/__init__.py chooses Docker if docker_client.ping() succeeds and otherwise falls back to LocalCommandLineCodeExecutor after emitting a UserWarning.

behavior_summary: The dominant pattern is a sandbox that has the shape of isolation but not the substance: Docker and Azure are available paths, but the defaults the executor accepts make every path a sharp tool. create_default_code_executor() silently downgrades to LocalCommandLineCodeExecutor on any Docker failure (only a warnings.warn to stderr is interposed), LocalCommandLineCodeExecutor.__init__ itself merely warns — never gates — and copies the host's full os.environ into every child process, and DockerCommandLineCodeExecutor.start constructs the container with no user=, read_only=, mem_limit=, cap_drop=, pids_limit=, or network_mode="none" argument while bind-mounting the work directory as rw. Layered on top, no executor records a per-execution audit entry (timestamp, source agent, code digest, exit status) — logging.info/debug calls only cover infrastructure events like image pulls and container stop — so the remit's audit clause has no implementation anywhere in the subsystem.

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What every executor must always do
rules.rule_text: All file read and write operations MUST be confined to a configured work directory, and any path resolving outside it MUST be rejected.

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What every executor must always do
rules.rule_text: Every code execution MUST be subject to a configured wall-clock timeout, and processes that exceed it MUST be terminated.

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What every executor must always do
rules.rule_text: Stdout, stderr, and exit status MUST be captured completely and never silently discarded.

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What every executor must always do
rules.rule_text: The execution environment MUST be isolated from the host caller — a failing or malicious execution MUST NOT be able to read or modify the parent process's environment, credentials, or state.

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What every executor must always do
rules.rule_text: Persistence across executions MUST be limited to the configured state mechanism — preserved kernel state for Jupyter executors, work-directory files for command-line executors — and state MUST NEVER leak between unrelated sessions.

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What every executor must always do
rules.rule_text: Each execution MUST be recorded to an audit log capturing timestamp, executor kind, language, source agent, work directory, timeout, exit status, and a digest — not the body — of the executed code.

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What every executor must never do
rules.rule_text: Code MUST NOT be executed with host-level privileges when a less-privileged option achieves the same task.

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What every executor must never do
rules.rule_text: Instructions embedded in the code source that attempt to escape the sandbox, escalate privileges, or exfiltrate data MUST NOT be acted on.

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What every executor must never do
rules.rule_text: Work-directory confinement MUST NOT be bypassed under any condition, including symlinks, parent-directory traversal, absolute paths, or runtime-supplied volume mount overrides.

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What every executor must never do
rules.rule_text: Code MUST NOT be loaded or executed from remote URLs or unverified sources on the LLM's behalf — the LLM's generated code is the only accepted input.

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What every executor must never do
rules.rule_text: Error output MUST NOT be silently swallowed or transformed before it is returned to the caller.

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — What every executor must never do
rules.rule_text: The executor MUST NOT connect to services, databases, or networks not explicitly permitted by its configuration.

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — Human approval is required for
rules.rule_text: Use of the local host executor in production MUST require human approval — it runs code directly on the host OS without containerization; default production deployments should use a containerized executor, and local execution is acceptable only when the host is an ephemeral, isolated, operator-approved sandbox.

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — Human approval is required for
rules.rule_text: Enabling any network egress from the executor environment beyond a configured allow-list MUST require human approval.

rules.section: Behavioral Constraints — Human approval is required for
rules.rule_text: Mounting host volumes into the container executor at any path other than the configured work directory MUST require human approval.

findings.summary: LocalCommandLineCodeExecutor proceeds with host-level code execution after only a warnings.warn — no approval gate, no production-mode rejection.
findings.description: The constructor at local/__init__.py:163-169 issues a UserWarning recommending Docker and then unconditionally continues. There is no require_approval=True flag, no environment check, no input() confirmation; calling code that ignores the warning runs LLM-authored Python and shell directly on the host with the caller's full environment.
findings.policy_rule_text: Use of the local host executor in production MUST require human approval — it runs code directly on the host OS without containerization; default production deployments should use a containerized executor, and local execution is acceptable only when the host is an ephemeral, isolated, operator-approved sandbox.

evidence.snippet: warnings.warn("Using LocalCommandLineCodeExecutor may execute code on the local machine which can be unsafe...", UserWarning, stacklevel=2) — control surface, lines 163-169
asyncio.create_subprocess_exec runs the LLM-authored file directly with the constructed env at lines 426-435 — no approval interposition between warn and exec

recommended_actions: Replace the warnings.warn call at local/__init__.py:163 with a constructor argument require_approval: bool (default True) that raises RuntimeError unless the caller passes acknowledged_local_execution=True and an AUTOGEN_LOCAL_EXECUTOR_APPROVED environment variable is set.
Refuse to start when the process detects production indicators (e.g., os.environ.get("AUTOGEN_ENV") == "production") regardless of the flag.

findings.summary: LocalCommandLineCodeExecutor copies the host's full os.environ into every executed subprocess, exposing all host secrets to LLM-authored code.
findings.description: At local/__init__.py:397 the executor calls env = os.environ.copy() and passes it to asyncio.create_subprocess_exec(..., env=env) at line 433. Any environment variable visible to the calling Python process — API keys, OAuth tokens, database URLs — is reachable from the LLM-generated code via os.environ, violating the remit's host-isolation clause.
findings.policy_rule_text: The execution environment MUST be isolated from the host caller — a failing or malicious execution MUST NOT be able to read or modify the parent process's environment, credentials, or state.

evidence.snippet: env = os.environ.copy()  # full host environment is inherited unconditionally
env=env passed into asyncio.create_subprocess_exec — child reads every host secret via os.environ

recommended_actions: Replace env = os.environ.copy() with a minimal allowlist (PATH, LANG, HOME plus any caller-supplied variables) and require explicit opt-in for --inherit-env.
Document the secret-exposure risk in the LocalCommandLineCodeExecutor docstring at the danger admonition.

findings.summary: No executor records a per-execution audit entry — the remit's audit-log clause has no implementation anywhere in the subsystem.
findings.description: The remit requires every execution to log timestamp, executor kind, language, source agent, work directory, timeout, exit status, and a code digest. A grep of the in-scope tree shows only 22 logging.* calls, all of which describe infrastructure events (image pulls, container stop, kill-command scheduling, temp-file cleanup); no call captures the per-execution fields, and no separate audit sink exists.
findings.policy_rule_text: Each execution MUST be recorded to an audit log capturing timestamp, executor kind, language, source agent, work directory, timeout, exit status, and a digest — not the body — of the executed code.

evidence.snippet: _execute_code_dont_check_setup (lines 341-474) computes a sha256 hash of code at line 381 for filename use but never emits it to a log; no audit record is produced before, during, or after the subprocess.run.
_execute_code_dont_check_setup (lines 327-376) writes the file, runs the in-container command, and returns — no audit-shaped logging call is made.

recommended_actions: Add a structured logging.getLogger("autogen.code_executor.audit").info(...) call at the start and end of each execute_code_blocks implementation, emitting a JSON record with the eight remit-mandated fields.
Document the logger name in the README so operators can route it to a separate file or sink.

findings.summary: DockerCommandLineCodeExecutor creates containers with no user=, read_only=, cap_drop=, mem_limit=, or pids_limit= argument — containers run as root with full capabilities.
findings.description: The client.containers.create(...) call in start() at docker/_docker_code_executor.py:537-550 passes only image, name, entrypoint, command, tty, detach, auto_remove, volumes, working_dir, extra_hosts, and device_requests. No least-privilege flags are set, so the default python:3-slim image runs as UID 0 with all Linux capabilities and no memory or process ceiling — defeating the "less-privileged option" remit clause for the container path.
findings.policy_rule_text: Code MUST NOT be executed with host-level privileges when a less-privileged option achieves the same task.

evidence.snippet: client.containers.create(self._image, name=..., entrypoint=..., command=command, tty=True, detach=True, auto_remove=self._auto_remove, volumes={...}, working_dir="/workspace", extra_hosts=..., device_requests=...) — no user/cap_drop/read_only/mem_limit/pids_limit; lines 537-550

recommended_actions: Default the create call to user="1000:1000", cap_drop=["ALL"], read_only=False only when the work directory needs writes (consider tmpfs for /tmp), mem_limit="512m", and pids_limit=128, with constructor arguments to relax each.
Document a SecureContainerProfile dataclass and pass it through DockerCommandLineCodeExecutorConfig so the hardened defaults are visible in serialized configs.

findings.summary: Docker containers and the docker-jupyter server start with no network_mode="none" — LLM-authored code has full outbound network reach by default.
findings.description: Both DockerCommandLineCodeExecutor.start() (docker/_docker_code_executor.py:537-550) and DockerJupyterServer.__init__ (docker_jupyter/_jupyter_server.py:363-372) omit network_mode, so the container inherits the default bridge network with outbound internet access. Code executed inside the container can curl, dial, and exfiltrate without ever crossing an executor-level egress check — violating both the network-permission rule and the "approval required for egress" rule.
findings.policy_rule_text: The executor MUST NOT connect to services, databases, or networks not explicitly permitted by its configuration. / Enabling any network egress from the executor environment beyond a configured allow-list MUST require human approval.

evidence.snippet: containers.create lacks network_mode; container joins the default bridge with outbound DNS+TCP, lines 537-550
containers.run also lacks network_mode and additionally sets publish_all_ports=True at line 368

recommended_actions: Default network_mode="none" in both container starts; add a network_allowlist: list[str] constructor argument that flips to network_mode="bridge" and installs an iptables egress filter inside the container's init_command.
Remove publish_all_ports=True from _jupyter_server.py:368 and expose only the kernelgateway port the client actually needs.

findings.summary: get_file_name_from_content enforces work-directory containment for # filename: headers but no executor validates volumes, symlinks, or absolute paths the executed code itself opens.
findings.description: The check at _common.py:96-111 resolves the declared filename and calls path.relative_to(workspace_path.resolve()), which correctly rejects traversal for the wrapper file. Once execution starts, the script can write to any path the process can reach — the work-directory confinement claim in the remit is enforced only for the harness's own bookkeeping, not for the LLM's outputs.
findings.policy_rule_text: All file read and write operations MUST be confined to a configured work directory, and any path resolving outside it MUST be rejected. / Work-directory confinement MUST NOT be bypassed under any condition, including symlinks, parent-directory traversal, absolute paths, or runtime-supplied volume mount overrides.

evidence.snippet: get_file_name_from_content only validates the first-line '# filename:' hint via relative_to; the function body's own open() calls are not policed (lines 96-111)
volumes={str(self.bind_dir.resolve()): {'bind': '/workspace', 'mode': 'rw'}, **self._extra_volumes} — extra_volumes is merged without traversal validation

recommended_actions: In DockerCommandLineCodeExecutor.__init__, validate every key in extra_volumes against an allowed_host_paths allowlist and reject path strings containing .. or symlink targets outside that list.
Document that the local and Jupyter executors do not confine post-execution file I/O and steer operators to the containerized paths when confinement matters.

findings.summary: create_default_code_executor() silently downgrades Docker→Local on any Docker failure with only a UserWarning between the two paths.
findings.description: When _is_docker_available() returns False or the DockerCommandLineCodeExecutor(...) constructor raises (caught by except Exception: pass at code_executors/__init__.py:64-66), the function falls through to LocalCommandLineCodeExecutor(...) after a warnings.warn at lines 69-75. A caller that built around the helper for safety gets host-level execution as soon as Docker hiccups — and the warning may be the only signal anywhere in the operator's log.
findings.policy_rule_text: Use of the local host executor in production MUST require human approval — it runs code directly on the host OS without containerization; default production deployments should use a containerized executor, and local execution is acceptable only when the host is an ephemeral, isolated, operator-approved sandbox.

evidence.snippet: except Exception: pass — Docker init failures fall through to LocalCommandLineCodeExecutor at line 78 with only a warnings.warn at line 69
return LocalCommandLineCodeExecutor(work_dir=work_dir) — silent downgrade path

recommended_actions: Change the fallback at code_executors/__init__.py:78-80 to raise RuntimeError("Docker unavailable; pass allow_local_fallback=True to opt in") unless a new allow_local_fallback argument is supplied.
When fallback is permitted, emit a logging.warning at the named logger autogen.code_executor.fallback so the event lands in operator logs, not just stderr.

findings.summary: DockerCommandLineCodeExecutor accepts arbitrary extra_volumes and extra_hosts mounts from the constructor without an approval flag.
findings.description: The constructor at docker/_docker_code_executor.py:177-178 takes extra_volumes: Optional[Dict[str, Dict[str, str]]] and extra_hosts: Optional[Dict[str, str]] and merges them straight into containers.create at line 546, 548. The remit requires human approval for any mount outside the work directory; there is no flag, no confirmation, and no allowlist between operator input and the Docker call.
findings.policy_rule_text: Mounting host volumes into the container executor at any path other than the configured work directory MUST require human approval.

evidence.snippet: extra_volumes and extra_hosts taken as plain dicts, merged into containers.create at line 546-548 with no allowlist

recommended_actions: Add an allow_extra_mounts: bool = False flag to DockerCommandLineCodeExecutor.__init__ that must be True before extra_volumes or extra_hosts is honored, and raise otherwise.

findings.summary: JupyterCodeExecutor.restart() is the only state reset — Jupyter kernels otherwise persist all variables across execute_code_blocks calls with no per-session boundary.
findings.description: JupyterCodeExecutor (jupyter/_jupyter_code_executor.py:48-335) reuses the same NotebookClient across calls; DockerJupyterCodeExecutor (docker_jupyter/_docker_jupyter.py:158-302) reuses the same kernel id. The remit limits persistence to "preserved kernel state for Jupyter executors" but adds "state MUST NEVER leak between unrelated sessions"; the executor has no notion of a session boundary beyond an explicit restart() call, and the caller must remember to invoke it.
findings.policy_rule_text: Persistence across executions MUST be limited to the configured state mechanism — preserved kernel state for Jupyter executors, work-directory files for command-line executors — and state MUST NEVER leak between unrelated sessions.

evidence.snippet: restart() is the only state-reset call; no session_id or per-caller binding exists, lines 268-271
restart() reuses the same kernel id; nothing in execute_code_blocks resets state implicitly

recommended_actions: Add an optional session_id constructor parameter and require the caller to pass it on every execute_code_blocks call; restart the kernel automatically when the value changes.
Document the persistence guarantee in the docstring so operators know they must call restart() between unrelated callers.

findings.summary: LocalCommandLineCodeExecutor swallows the file-cleanup OSError and logs only — temp files that survive cleanup leave artifacts the audit log cannot see.
findings.description: At local/__init__.py:467-472 the cleanup loop wraps file.unlink(missing_ok=True) in a try/except that calls logging.error and continues; the function still returns a successful CommandLineCodeResult even though the cleanup-temp-files contract failed. Combined with the absent per-execution audit log, an operator has no record that a temp file lingered — a forensic blind spot rather than a data-loss bug.
findings.policy_rule_text: Error output MUST NOT be silently swallowed or transformed before it is returned to the caller.

evidence.snippet: except OSError as error: logging.error(...) — the cleanup failure does not surface to the CommandLineCodeResult or trigger an audit entry, lines 467-472

recommended_actions: Surface cleanup failures as a non-fatal warning on the returned CommandLineCodeResult.output (e.g., append \n[warning] temp file <path> survived cleanup: <err>).
When the audit-logger from PRAX-2026-05-25-003 lands, emit a cleanup_failure event with the path and error.

findings.summary: DockerCommandLineCodeExecutor defaults to the unpinned python:3-slim tag — image identity drifts between every container start without a digest pin.
findings.description: At docker/_docker_code_executor.py:71 and :160 the config and constructor default to image="python:3-slim"; when the image is missing it is pulled at :524 without an integrity check. A registry compromise or a maintainer-side tag re-push silently changes the runtime base of every executor instance — the remit-isolated supply chain has no integrity boundary.

evidence.snippet: image: str = 'python:3-slim' — no digest, no signature verification; pulled at line 524 with client.images.pull and used directly

recommended_actions: Default the image to a digest-pinned form (e.g., python:3-slim@sha256:<digest>) and document the supported-tag rotation cadence in the README.
Add an optional image_digest_allowlist constructor argument that fails fast if the resolved image digest is not in the list.

findings.summary: DockerJupyterServer calls os.chmod(bind_dir, 0o777) on the host bind directory before mounting it into the container.
findings.description: At docker_jupyter/_jupyter_server.py:328 the constructor chmods the operator-supplied bind_dir to world-writable to work around UID mismatches between the container's NB_USER and the host caller. The remit does not authorize the executor to alter host file modes on the caller's behalf, and the resulting 0o777 directory is reachable by every local user on the host for the lifetime of the container.

evidence.snippet: os.chmod(bind_dir, 0o777) — world-writable host directory created unconditionally when bind_dir is supplied

recommended_actions: Replace the 0o777 chmod with 0o770 and only set the group to match the container's NB_GID, or document the requirement and refuse to touch the host mode at all.

findings.summary: JupyterCodeExecutor and DockerJupyterCodeExecutor accept an arbitrary output_dir with no traversal guard.
findings.description: jupyter/_jupyter_code_executor.py:148 and docker_jupyter/_docker_jupyter.py:175-179 take output_dir and call Path(output_dir).mkdir(exist_ok=True, parents=True) directly. A caller-supplied path with .. segments is honored verbatim — the work-directory containment that _common.get_file_name_from_content enforces for # filename: hints is not applied here.

evidence.snippet: self._output_dir = Path(...) ; self._output_dir.mkdir(exist_ok=True, parents=True) — no resolve+relative_to check

recommended_actions: Validate output_dir against a configured base path with Path(output_dir).resolve().relative_to(base.resolve()) and raise ValueError on traversal.

positives.title: Work-directory containment for inline filename hints
positives.description: _common.get_file_name_from_content resolves the declared filename and rejects anything outside the workspace via path.relative_to(workspace_path.resolve()) — a real, code-level containment check, not a prompt-level one.

positives.title: Wall-clock timeouts wired through every executor
positives.description: Every executor accepts a timeout: int constructor argument (validated >= 1), wraps execution in asyncio.wait_for or invokes the in-container timeout binary at docker/_docker_code_executor.py:360, and surfaces a 124 exit code on overrun — the remit's timeout-and-terminate rule is the most consistently implemented control in scope.

positives.title: Cancellation propagation via CancellationToken.link_future
positives.description: Each execute_code_blocks implementation links the in-flight asyncio task to the caller's cancellation token, so a caller-side cancel can terminate the subprocess or kernel call cleanly; Docker additionally schedules a pkill -f against the running command via the kept event-loop reference.

positives.title: Container auto_remove and stop_container defaults
positives.description: DockerCommandLineCodeExecutor defaults both auto_remove and stop_container to True, registering an asyncio_atexit cleanup, so a forgotten executor does not leak a long-lived container.

log_files.no_logs_note: No execution-history or audit log files exist in the scanned scope; the absence is the substance of PRAX-2026-05-25-003.

raise_posture.weighted_rationale: Ad hoc. The subsystem ships meaningful operative controls — work-directory confinement via path.relative_to(workspace_path.resolve()) in _common.get_file_name_from_content, configurable wall-clock timeouts wired through asyncio.wait_for and the in-container timeout binary, and four isolation tiers more secure than direct host execution — but every default sits at the most permissive end of its range: Docker containers run as root with no resource caps and full network, the local executor inherits the parent process environment, and the create_default helper silently picks Local when Docker is missing. Zero Trust and Monitor Continuously land at 1 — there is no approval gate or audit log anywhere — and Build an AI Red Team scores 0 with no adversarial-testing artifact present in scope.

categories.name: Limit Your Domain
categories.rationale: SUPPORTED_LANGUAGES on each executor restricts the surface to Python plus a small shell family (bash, sh, pwsh, powershell, ps1) and Azure narrows further to python; this is a real, code-level domain limit but does not constrain what the executed Python or shell can do once admitted.

categories.name: Balance Your Knowledge Base
categories.rationale: The executor accepts code only from the calling AutoGen agent — there is no fetch-and-execute path from URLs or remote sources — but the local executor copies the entire host os.environ into the child process (local/__init__.py:397), pushing every host secret into the executed code's reachable knowledge base.

categories.name: Implement Zero Trust
categories.rationale: Code from the LLM is executed verbatim with no per-block approval gate on any executor, the Docker container is started with no user=/read_only=/mem_limit=/cap_drop=/network_mode= constraints (docker/_docker_code_executor.py:537-550), and the local executor's only "guard" is a warnings.warn call (local/__init__.py:163-169) that proceeds unconditionally.

categories.name: Manage Your Supply Chain
categories.rationale: The default Docker image is the unpinned tag python:3-slim (docker/_docker_code_executor.py:71, 160) and DockerJupyterServer builds from a Dockerfile that pulls quay.io/jupyter/docker-stacks-foundation with no digest (docker_jupyter/_jupyter_server.py:267-287); the subsystem itself uses pinned Python dependencies via the AutoGen extras model, but the runtime layer that the executor isolates against has no integrity pinning.

categories.name: Build an AI Red Team
categories.rationale: Nothing in the in-scope code_executors/ and autogen_core/code_executor/ directories shows adversarial testing of the sandbox boundary — no escape tests, no fixtures exercising # filename: traversal, no red-team artifacts of any kind; the test suite that exists for these executors lives outside scope, and within scope absence is the only observation.

categories.name: Monitor Continuously
categories.rationale: The 22 logging.* calls in scope cover infrastructure plumbing (image pulls, container stop, kill-command scheduling, file-cleanup errors) and never record what was executed: no executor emits a structured entry containing timestamp, source agent, work directory, code digest, and exit status the remit explicitly requires.
